sound_type: accelerating stone-fall percussion -- initial impact crack through full cascade of overlapping impacts, valley amplification, E=7
ecosystem: Cliff face -- Cascade canyon walls, talus slopes, road cuts in high-relief volcanic terrain
season: Episodic (year-round; spring peak March-May due to freeze-thaw cycle completion and melt lubrication)
cascade_geology_context: Juan de Fuca subduction → tectonic uplift + volcanism. Volcanic rock joint systems as natural fracture planes. Freeze-thaw weathering (9% ice expansion). Canyon walls as inherently unstable exposed slope faces.

acoustic_physics.phase_1_initial: First rock impact: sharp, dry, percussive. Higher frequency than ice fracture. Stone-on-stone: dense, hard acoustic quality. Single struck note before acceleration.
acoustic_physics.phase_2_acceleration: Fracture multiplies fragments → impacts multiply → percussion accelerates. Time between impacts decreases as more events occur simultaneously. The defining acoustic characteristic.
acoustic_physics.phase_3_full_cascade: Continuous overlapping percussion. Large blocks: deep, resonant. Small fragments: high-frequency clatter. Gravity as drummer.
acoustic_physics.phase_4_tail: Individual rocks and clatter continue in aftermath. Periodic impacts against fading echo background. Settles over seconds to minutes.
acoustic_physics.valley_amplification: Canyon walls reflect and amplify. Narrow canyon = more reverberant, enclosed. Open slope = less reverberant, more diffuse.
acoustic_physics.fundamental_frequency_initial_hz: 500-3000 Hz for initial hard impact crack
acoustic_physics.fundamental_frequency_cascade_hz: 50-2000 Hz for full cascade (large blocks to small fragments)

nodes.title: Initial Impact -- Stone on Stone
nodes.description: First rock block contacts cliff face below. Sharp, dry, percussive. Higher frequency than ice fracture. Dense quality. The rockslide announces itself with a single struck note before accelerating.

nodes.title: Acceleration Phase -- Percussion Multiplying
nodes.description: Fracture multiplies fragments; impacts multiply; percussion accelerates. The defining characteristic. Gravity as the rhythm that accelerates toward maximum.

nodes.title: Cascade Geology -- Uplift vs. Erosion
nodes.description: Juan de Fuca subduction → uplift. Volcanic rock joint systems → fracture planes. Freeze-thaw → fracture widening. The rockslide is geology made audible.

nodes.title: Spring Peak -- Prepared Failures Release
nodes.description: Winter freeze-thaw prepares; spring melt releases. March-May peak. WSDOT SR-20 reopening involves debris clearing. Episodic event with seasonal pattern.

nodes.title: Mass-Movement Trilogy -- 265-267
nodes.description: 265 (glacier calving, ice, E=6) → 266 (rockslide, rock, E=7) → 267 (mudslide, saturated soil, E=8). Energy escalates. Duration increases. Three Cascade mass-movement mechanisms.

cross_references.relationship: Direct predecessor in mass-movement trilogy. Both crack + cascade. Ice (E=6) vs rock (E=7). Energy escalates from ice to rock.
Direct successor. Saturated soil, E=8. Arc completes: ice → rock → soil. Three acoustic languages of Cascade mass movement.
Triggering weather. Intense precipitation saturates rock fractures. Thunderstorm and rockslide connected by same hydrological system.
Biology returns at 268 after three mass-movement degrees. Maximum contrast: dry percussive rockslide cascade vs. Pacific Wren's amplified rain-day song.
